text.title: verb_object names with namespace prefix
text.summary: The concrete rule for tool names: verb_object form, lowercase snake_case, prefixed by service namespace whenever multiple servers expose similar verbs.

section.title: The rule
section.p: Tool names are part of the prompt. The model selects by name first, description second. A vague or ambiguous name forces the model to read every description on every turn, which collapses selection accuracy as the catalog grows.

rule.statement: Every tool name MUST be lowercase snake_case in the form `verb_object` (e.g., `create_issue`, `search_files`). When the agent sees tools from more than one service or MCP server, prepend a service namespace (`github_create_issue`, `stripe_refund_charge`).
rule.rationale: Anthropic's internal evals show prefix and naming schemes meaningfully change selection accuracy; verb_object mirrors CRUD endpoint conventions Claude saw millions of times in pre-training, while namespaces eliminate name collisions inside MCP aggregators.

example.note: Verb is unambiguous, object is explicit, namespace eliminates collision with `asana_*` and any future `gitlab_*`.
`search` collides with every other server's `search` in a gateway. `manage` hides three verbs behind one name — the model must guess. `do_thing` carries zero signal.

antipattern.description: Polymorphic verb tools that take an `action` enum (`manage_files(action="copy"|"move"|"delete", ...)`).
antipattern.reason: The model has to read the description and remember which kwargs are valid per action. Atomic verbs (`copy_file`, `move_file`, `delete_file`) are self-describing and have typed schemas the runtime can validate.
Long names burn context budget, dilute the verb, and hurt lexical match with the user's request.

section.reference: Pre-ship checklist for any new tool name.
